exemplar.title: The effect of milk fat percentage and tea temperature on the formation of a film layer on tea

detailedExemplarData.title: The effect of milk fat percentage and tea temperature on the formation of a film layer on tea

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Title clearly states the independent and dependent variables, giving immediate clarity on focus
Well-structured explanation of proteinâtannin interactions and denaturation mechanisms
Rationale for selected temperature range is justified by denaturation kinetics and evaporation considerations
Comprehensive control of variables (tea type, steeping time, container) ensures consistency
Iterative approach demonstrated through preliminary tests and parameter adjustments
weaknesses: Research question phrased as a statement rather than a clear interrogative
Background lacks specific justification for chosen temperatures and fat levels
Method omits number of replicates, leading to reproducibility ambiguities
Qualitative film assessment lacks objective criteria or measurement scale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand in Research Design aligns with the 3â4 band. The student situates their question within a broad biochemical context, describes key methodological considerations and provides a step-by-step method with few ambiguities. However, contextualisation is not specific enough, considerations are descriptive rather than explanatory, and reproducibility is hindered by missing replicate details and qualitative scoring. These collectively justify a mid-band score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Data recording includes temperatures, fat percentages and qualitative comments, indicating at least minimal data capture
weaknesses: Results table is poorly formatted with unclear headings and units misaligned
No graphical representation or organized qualitative scale was provided
Limited discussion of measurement uncertainties or observer error
Data processing remains purely narrative with no quantitative or statistical treatment
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Data Analysis strands fall at the bottom of the 1â2 band: the recording and processing are communicated but neither clear nor precise; evidence of uncertainty consideration is very limited; and data processing is minimal and qualitative with major omissions. This clearly warrants the lowest band mark of 1 out of 6.

strengths: Conclusion directly addresses the research question by linking higher temperature and fat content to film formation
Relevance to the question is clear and focused
weaknesses: Conclusion is not fully supported by quantitative analysis or specific data thresholds
Comparison to accepted scientific context remains superficial with no citation of quantitative studies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is relevant but only loosely supported by the qualitative observations, placing it in the 1â2 band. The comparison to scientific context is also superficial, confirming the lower band. Given the stronger performance on relevance (upper end of the band) but weaker context comparison, a score of 2 out of 6 appropriately reflects the overall quality.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (e.g., low tannin content, subjective observation) and links them to film stability
Proposes realistic improvements (tea-to-milk ratio adjustments, vessel shape, stirring control) directly addressing limitations
Evaluation demonstrates comprehensive consideration of additional variables like pH and mineral content
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact or prioritise which weaknesses most affected outcomes
Practical constraints of proposed improvements are not fully discussed
Improvement rationales are described but lack deeper explanation or justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both Evaluation strands are described at a solid mid-band level (3â4). The student specifies realistic improvements and methodological weaknesses in context but stops short of ranking their impact or explaining each improvement in depth. This consistency across strands justifies a score of 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a conclusionâthat higher temperature and higher milk fat promote film formation but films are unstableâwhich is clearly relevant to the research question.  The reasoning is loosely linked to the qualitative observations, yet the scant dataset and absence of quantified evidence mean the conclusion is only partially supported.  This aligns with a ârelevant but not supported by the analysisâ judgment, awarding the upper point of the 1â2 band.

Data are communicated in a single, poorly formatted table mixing temperature, fat percentage, milk volume and descriptive comments.  Headings are unclear, units appear inside cells, and duplication of the trial number obscures the dataset.  No graphs or organised qualitative scales are presented.  Recording is therefore communicated, but neither clearly nor precisely, matching the lowest achievement band.

Processing is confined to a qualitative âfilm/no film/patchyâ narrative; no quantitative scale, frequency counts or statistical treatment is attempted.  As such, some minimal processing relevant to the question is present but with major omissions and no numerical analysis, placing the work at the bottom of the 1â2 band.

The student identifies concrete methodological weaknessesâlow tannin content of Lipton tea, subjective visual observation, dilution by high milk volumes, vessel geometry, stirring disturbancesâand links each to how it may have limited film stability or detection.  While the discussion stops short of quantitatively ranking their relative impact, the weaknesses are specific and described in context, matching the middle band (3â4).

The discussion references accepted ideas about tannin-protein interactions and the role of fat, but the comparison remains superficial and descriptive; there is no specific engagement with published quantitative findings or mechanisms beyond reiterating textbook statements.  This corresponds to a superficial comparison, meriting the lower end of the 1â2 band.

The student provides a concise but relevant literature-based overview of tannin-protein interactions, protein denaturation, water hardness and fat globules before stating the research question.  Although the coverage is not exhaustive, it does link directly to the stated aim of studying milk-tea film formation and therefore places the question in an identifiable biochemical context.  The explanation, however, is still fairly general and does not narrow down to the exact experimental system (e.g., no reference to why the three particular temperatures or fat levels were chosen in relation to known thresholds).  This situates the work securely in the âoutlined within a broad contextâ level rather than the fully specific contextualisation demanded for the top band.

There is no explicit mention of temperature measurement uncertainty, no range for milk volumes, and no discussion of observer error in judging film presence.  The only implied uncertainty is the repeat measurement of temperature after milk addition.  This constitutes âlimited evidenceâ of consideration rather than none at all, so the strand falls in the 1â2 mark band at the lower end.

Improvementsâaltering tea-to-milk ratio, increasing tannin concentration, minimising disturbance, using a wider vesselâdirectly address the identified weaknesses and are feasible.  The student explains how each change could enhance film formation, but does not fully justify which would have the greatest effect or discuss practical constraints, so the strand fits comfortably within the âdescribedâ (not fully âexplainedâ) 3â4 band.

Key methodological factorsâtea temperature, milk fat percentage, volumes, steeping time, vessel type and safetyâare identified and the rationale for controlling or varying them is described.  The student recognises that protein denaturation, tannin concentration and evaporation could influence the dependent variable, demonstrating an understanding of how each variable relates to data sufficiency.  Nevertheless, the considerations remain descriptive; they are not extended to an explanation of how large a temperature difference is required, why single trials were deemed sufficient or how qualitative scoring will ensure enough data, so the work fits the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than âexplained.â

A step-by-step method is supplied that includes water volume, steeping time, temperature readings, milk addition and cooling period, enabling another investigator to repeat the basic procedure.  However, ambiguities persist: only one trial per condition is actually performed despite the plan for repeats, the use of three different milk volumes conflicts with the stated factor of interest, and the qualitative film assessment lacks objective criteria.  These omissions mean the protocol is âreproducible with few ambiguitiesâ rather than fully reproducible, warranting placement in the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly states the independent and dependent variables, giving immediate clarity on the investigationâs focus.
The table of contents uses unconventional mathematical notation (e.g., overlines), which may confuse readers; simplify page numbering for clarity.
The âContentsâ header is formatted inconsistently; consider standardizing fonts and styles to improve professionalism.
Background on water hardness contextualizes the study well, but it would benefit from specifying why Norwegian water characteristics were chosen over other options.
The explanation of proteinâtannin interactions and denaturation mechanisms is well structured and directly relevant to film formation.
The research question is phrased as a statement and could be reframed as a clear interrogative to meet scientific convention.
The hypothesis is clear but lacks operational definitions for âstabilityâ and âthicknessâ; add specific criteria to enable consistent assessment.
The rationale for the selected temperature range is well justified by denaturation kinetics and evaporation considerations.
The method for assessing film formation is purely visual; introduce an objective scale or micrometer measurement to quantify thickness.
Controlled variables are comprehensively managed, ensuring that tea type, steeping time and container remain consistent across trials.
The equipment list contains nested indentations that impair readability; streamline entries for a clear, linear list.
Conducting a preliminary test and adjusting parameters demonstrates a thoughtful iterative approach to optimizing the design.
Details on milk addition omit repeat counts for each condition; specify number of replicates to ensure reproducibility.
The protocol states only one trial per combination, limiting statistical power and obscuring variability.
The trial breakdown header is unclear; restructure to indicate exactly how temperature and volume are combined in each trial.
The results tableâs headings and units are misaligned and ambiguous; reformat columns with clear labels, units and row grouping.
Consider adding footnotes or color coding in the table to clarify repeated trial numbers and narrative entries.
Include a scale bar or ruler in the figure to allow assessment of film thickness from the photo.
The narrative of results remains qualitative; analyse frequency or duration of film presence numerically to strengthen your analysis.
The conclusion restates observations but lacks direct linkage to quantitative data; integrate actual temperature and fat thresholds.
Comparison to accepted scientific context remains superficial; cite specific studies or quantitative findings on tannin-protein film formation.
Discussing pH, mineral content and stirring factors demonstrates a comprehensive evaluation of additional variables affecting film stability.
Identifying consistency in brewing and control of environmental conditions is a strong evaluation of methodological strengths.
The evaluation of low tannin content in Lipton tea is specific and well connected to its effect on film formation.
Proposed adjustments to tea-to-milk ratio, vessel shape and stirring are realistic and directly address identified limitations.
Ensure the references section uses a consistent citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) and include DOIs or access dates uniformly.
